pattern: Every cell on either diagonal of a square matrix satisfies row == col or row + col == n - 1, so a single pass summing cells that match either condition -- counting the middle cell once -- gets both diagonals in one loop.
hints: You don't need to walk the matrix twice, once per diagonal -- a cell is on the main diagonal when its row index equals its column index, and on the anti-diagonal when row + col equals the last valid index.
Loop over row indices 0..n-1 and add mat[i][i] and mat[i][n-1-i] to a running total each time.
When n is odd, the very center cell has i == n-1-i, so adding both mat[i][i] and mat[i][n-1-i] would double count it -- subtract it once, or only add the anti-diagonal term when i != n-1-i.

edge_inputs.description: 3x3 matrix with a distinct center cell
4x4 matrix, all identical values, no shared center cell
1x1 matrix, the single cell is on both diagonals but counted once
2x2 matrix, smallest even size, no shared center cell
5x5 matrix, odd size, verifies the true center cell isn't double counted
3x3 with distinct max values placed only on the diagonals, off-diagonal cells at max value 100 to catch code that sums the whole matrix by mistake
all cells at maximum constraint value 100, largest possible sum for a small matrix

stress.description: random n x n matrices across a range of sizes with random cell values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    mat = [[rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [mat]
